_cite.reason: URL-normalization 1c: strip trailing markdown-emphasis `*`/backtick glued to citation URLs in intelligence_item_sections.content_md (customer-checkable link + validate_item_provenance criterion-2 exact-compare). Operator ruling 2026-07-04. Same transform as the write-site fix.

prior.content_md:
Local Law 97 targets building owners and operators — the parties legally responsible for a building's annual greenhouse gas emissions. Within the LL97 supply chain, the NYC Department of Buildings (DOB) sits as the primary regulator, with building owners bearing the direct legal obligation to calculate, certify, and report annual GHG emissions and to remain within the applicable cap for their building identification number (BIN). The workspace, as a freight forwarder, sits in this chain in two distinct ways.

First, to the extent the workspace **occupies or operates** any NYC facility over 25,000 gross square feet (warehouses, cargo terminals, offices, film/TV production stages, fine art or luxury goods storage), it may qualify as the building owner or, if leasing, as a tenant whose energy consumption directly drives the landlord's reported emissions. In either case, the workspace's energy use — from HVAC, lighting, dock operations, and climate-control systems critical for live event equipment, perishable cargo, or fine art — is the principal input to the building's annual compliance calculation. The workspace should obtain legal confirmation of whether it is classified as 'owner,' 'authorized agent,' or 'tenant' under LL97, as this determines whether it must retain a Registered Design Professional (RDP) or Retro-Commissioning Agent (RCxA) to certify the annual emissions report.

Second, to the extent the workspace advises clients (shippers, producers, brands) who own or occupy covered NYC buildings, it may serve in an advisory capacity — helping those clients understand how cargo dwell time, temperature control requirements, and warehouse energy intensity interact with the building's LL97 cap. Landlords in NYC are increasingly inserting lease clauses allocating emissions costs to tenants proportionate to their energy share, making this a direct operational cost exposure for any workspace leasing space in a covered building. Role placement requiring legal confirmation: whether the workspace qualifies as 'owner' or 'authorized agent' able to file reports, and whether any multi-building tax-lot scenario (50,000 sq ft aggregate threshold) captures facilities not individually above the 25,000 sq ft single-building threshold.

*Source: NYC DOB LL97 Greenhouse Gas Emissions Reduction Page, NYC Department of Buildings, March 2026. https://www.nyc.gov/site/buildings/codes/ll97-greenhouse-gas-emissions-reductions.page; LegalClarity NYC LL97 Analysis, LegalClarity, May 2026. https://legalclarity.org/what-is-nyc-local-law-97-requirements-and-penalties/*
